report.cost_of_living: Toronto rent is high relative to income, particularly downtown, but shared housing and suburbs offer more affordable options. Daily expenses are comparable to other major North American cities.
report.academic: Business programs are competitive with a strong emphasis on group projects, case studies, and networking events. Coursework is manageable but success depends heavily on proactive engagement with career services.
report.visa: Study permits are relatively straightforward, and Canada offers a well-defined post-graduation work permit pathway, making the visa process one of the more student-friendly in the world.
report.culture_shock: Toronto is highly multicultural, which softens culture shock significantly. Social norms are generally informal and welcoming, though winters can be a real adjustment for students from warmer climates.
report.community: Toronto has an extremely large and active international student and diaspora community across nearly every background, with abundant student clubs and cultural associations.
report.career: Business graduates have solid access to finance, consulting, and tech-adjacent roles in Toronto's large economy, though competition for top-tier roles is intense.
report.safety: Toronto is generally safe with normal big-city precautions; downtown areas are busy but low-violent-crime relative to comparable-sized US cities.
report.climate: Cold, snowy winters from November to March and warm, pleasant summers; the winter adjustment is often cited as the most significant lifestyle change for new arrivals.
report.part_time_work: International students can work up to 24 hours per week off-campus during studies, which is generous and commonly used to gain local work experience and offset costs.

health: Cold winters and short daylight can reduce energy and make commuting harder.
Part-time work plus group projects can lead to sleep loss if overloaded.
Shared housing farther from campus may increase commute fatigue.
mood: Toronto's multicultural community can improve belonging if the player joins clubs.
Competitive networking culture can feel stressful and socially draining.
Winter isolation is a real adaptation challenge for students from warmer climates.
money: High rent creates constant budget pressure, especially downtown.
Part-time work and internships can improve finances and local experience.
Networking events, transit, winter clothing, and case competitions add hidden costs.
city_major_specific_challenges: Choose between a downtown apartment near networking events and cheaper suburban housing.
Balance case competition preparation against paid work shifts.
Spend money on professional clothes and events to improve business career prospects.
Manage winter fatigue during midterms and internship recruitment season.
